resources.title: LC Column Care & Maintenance Guide
resources.summary: Best practices for extending HPLC and UHPLC column life, including storage, flushing protocols, and troubleshooting common pressure issues.
resources.content:
# LC Column Care & Maintenance Guide

Proper column care is essential for consistent chromatographic results and extended column life. This guide covers the fundamentals every lab should follow.

## Storage Best Practices

- **Short-term (overnight):** Leave column in mobile phase; cap both ends if disconnecting.
- **Long-term (>1 week):** Flush with appropriate storage solvent (typically 100% organic for reversed-phase columns). Cap tightly.
- **Never** store columns in buffers — salt crystallization will permanently damage the packing.

## Flushing Protocols

### Reversed-Phase Columns (C18, C8, Phenyl)
1. Flush with 10 column volumes of water to remove buffer salts
2. Transition to 50:50 water/acetonitrile (5 column volumes)
3. Flush with 100% acetonitrile (10 column volumes) for storage

### HILIC Columns
1. Flush with 10 column volumes of high-organic mobile phase
2. Store in 90:10 acetonitrile/water

## Troubleshooting High Back Pressure

| Symptom | Likely Cause | Solution |
|---------|-------------|----------|
| Sudden pressure spike | Blocked frit | Replace inlet frit or use inline filter |
| Gradual pressure increase | Particulate buildup | Back-flush column per manufacturer instructions |
| Pressure varies with temperature | Normal behavior | Ensure column oven is stable |

## Key Tips

- Always use an inline filter or guard column
- Filter and degas all mobile phases
- Avoid sudden changes in flow rate
- Record column usage (injections, hours) for tracking
- Follow manufacturer's pH and temperature limits

---

*Need help with column issues? [Contact Sierra Spectra](#contact) for expert troubleshooting support.*

resources.title: Mass Spectrometer Tuning Checklist
resources.summary: A step-by-step pre-run checklist for verifying MS tune parameters, ensuring optimal sensitivity and mass accuracy.
resources.content:
# Mass Spectrometer Tuning Checklist

Regular tuning verification ensures your mass spectrometer delivers reliable, reproducible data. Use this checklist before critical analyses.

## Pre-Tune Preparation

- [ ] Allow the instrument to warm up (minimum 30 minutes, ideally 1–2 hours)
- [ ] Verify vacuum levels are within specification
- [ ] Check gas supplies (nitrogen, argon) — ensure adequate pressure
- [ ] Inspect the ion source for contamination
- [ ] Verify calibrant solution is fresh and not expired

## Tuning Steps

### 1. Calibration
- Infuse calibration solution at recommended flow rate
- Run automatic calibration routine
- Verify mass accuracy is within ±0.1 Da (unit resolution) or ±5 ppm (high resolution)

### 2. Sensitivity Check
- Infuse sensitivity standard at known concentration
- Compare signal intensity to historical baseline
- Flag if sensitivity drops >30% from previous tune

### 3. Resolution Verification
- Check peak width at half-height
- Verify isotope pattern matches theoretical distribution
- Adjust resolution parameters if needed

### 4. Background Check
- Acquire background spectrum with solvent blank
- Check for contamination peaks (plasticizers, detergents)
- Common contaminants: m/z 391 (DEHP), m/z 279 (DBP)

## Post-Tune Documentation

- Save tune report with date and operator
- Record key parameters: sensitivity, resolution, mass accuracy
- Compare to acceptance criteria
- Log any corrective actions taken

## When to Re-Tune

- After source cleaning or maintenance
- After extended downtime (>1 week)
- When sensitivity drops below threshold
- After changing ionization mode (ESI ↔ APCI)
- Per your lab's SOP schedule

---

*Need help optimizing your MS performance? [Contact Sierra Spectra](#contact) for expert tuning and maintenance services.*

resources.title: Recommended PM Schedule for LC-MS Systems
resources.summary: Suggested preventive maintenance intervals for common LC-MS components to maximize uptime and data quality.
resources.content:
# Recommended PM Schedule for LC-MS Systems

A structured preventive maintenance program keeps your LC-MS running reliably and extends component life. Below are general guidelines — always consult your instrument's service manual for specific requirements.

## Daily Tasks

- Check solvent levels and waste containers
- Verify system pressures are within normal range
- Review system status indicators / error logs
- Inspect tubing connections for leaks

## Weekly Tasks

- Replace mobile phase if older than 5–7 days
- Clean autosampler needle and seat
- Inspect injection port seal
- Check gas supply pressures

## Monthly Tasks

- Replace inline solvent filters
- Clean or replace guard column
- Verify detector lamp hours (UV/PDA)
- Run system suitability test and compare to baseline
- Clean MS source exterior

## Quarterly Tasks

- Deep clean MS ion source (remove, sonicate, inspect)
- Replace pump seals if wear is detected
- Inspect and clean autosampler syringe
- Replace inlet frits
- Calibrate and tune MS

## Semi-Annual Tasks

- Replace pump check valves
- Replace deuterium lamp (UV detector) — or as hours dictate
- Full system qualification (OQ/PQ if regulated)
- Inspect all tubing for wear, kinks, or discoloration

## Annual Tasks

- Comprehensive PM by qualified service engineer
- Replace rough pump oil
- Inspect turbo pump bearings
- Full electronic diagnostics
- Update firmware/software if applicable

## Tracking Tips

- Maintain a logbook or spreadsheet for each instrument
- Record dates, tasks performed, parts replaced, and observations
- Set calendar reminders for upcoming PM tasks
- Track column usage and lamp hours

---

*Sierra Spectra offers customized PM contracts tailored to your lab's instruments and workload. [Contact us](#contact) to learn more.*
